coredata.dc:title: F and Na Co-doped carbon dots with enhanced afterglow emission for applications in fingerprint recognition and information encryption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Wei
Hu, Hao
Xue, Jinkun
Dong, Yongrun
Li, Wen
Zhao, Xia
Mao, Xiang
Li, Zequan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest as fluorescent nanomaterials; however, their long-afterglow emission properties remain underexplored. In this study, we synthesized dual-mode afterglow CDs exhibiting room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) via fluorine-sodium (F, Na) co-doping, achieving an extended afterglow lifetime of 574 ms. Under 365 nm UV excitation, the afterglow emission persists for 14 s (naked-eye observation) and 40 s (instrumentally quantified), demonstrating exceptional temporal resolution. Na doping enhances afterglow intensity by 11.3 %, while fluorine doping reduces the singlet–triplet energy gap (ΔEST) to 0.036 eV, facilitating efficient Reverse Intersystem Crossing and enabling TADF behavior. These synergistic effects position the developed CDs as promising candidates for anti-counterfeiting systems, multilevel data encryption, and high-security optical tagging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Element doping
Confinement
Room temperature phosphorescence
Thermally activated delayed fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: F, Na doping enhances afterglow and fluorescence intensity
The practicality of CDs composite materials

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AD23026201), and by the Industry-university Research Cooperation Fund of Guangxi Hongkai Furniture Co. Ltd & Guangxi University (20200782).

This research was funded by the Guangxi Science and Technology Program ( AD23026201 ), and by the Industry-university Research Cooperation Fund of Guangxi Hongkai Furniture Co., Ltd & Guangxi University ( 20200782 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of F,NCDs. (b) HRTEM image of F,NCDs. (c) Particle size distribution of F,NCDs. (d) XRD patterns of CDs-1 and matrix mixture. (e) FT-IR spectra of CDs. (f) XPS spectra of full scan of CDs.

(a) Digital photographs showing fluorescence and afterglow from CDs CDs-1. (b) Afterglow-time profile of CDs at 365 nm. (c) Afterglow-time (vision) of CDs(xNa). (d) Decay curves and lifetimes of CDs-1. (e) Variable excitation fluorescence spectrum of F,NCDs. (f) Variable excitation fluorescence spectrum of CDs-1. (g) Variable excitation afterglow spectrum of CDs-1.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs. High-resolution XPS of the CDs-1 (b) C 1s, (c) B 1s, (d) F 1s, (e) Plot of BCO2 versus CO content of CDs. (f) Variable temperature fluorescence spectrum (80–320 K) of CDs-1. (g) Variable-temperature spectral intensity versus temperature curve and variable-temperature fluorescence spectrum (80–320 K) of CDs-1. (h) Delayed-time afterglow spectra of CDs-1. (i) Energy level diagram of CDs-1.

(a) Schematic diagram of stability experiment. (b) Water stability (c) photostability (d) Thermogravimetric analysis of CDs-1 (e) Scanning calorimetry of CDs-1. (f) Comparison of afterglow performance.
(a) Fingerprint recognition (b) The correspondence between letters and signals (c) Information Encryption Response Program (d) Photo of Morse code information encryption response process.
(a) Formation Procedure for the CDs. (b) Afterglow Strategy for CDs.

head.ce:title: F and Na Co-doped carbon dots with enhanced afterglow emission for applications in fingerprint recognition and information encryption

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, Guangxi, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable interest as fluorescent nanomaterials; however, their long-afterglow emission properties remain underexplored. In this study, we synthesized dual-mode afterglow CDs exhibiting room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) via fluorine-sodium (F, Na) co-doping, achieving an extended afterglow lifetime of 574 ms. Under 365 nm UV excitation, the afterglow emission persists for 14 s (naked-eye observation) and 40 s (instrumentally quantified), demonstrating exceptional temporal resolution. Na doping enhances afterglow intensity by 11.3 %, while fluorine doping reduces the singlet–triplet energy gap (ΔEST) to 0.036 eV, facilitating efficient Reverse Intersystem Crossing and enabling TADF behavior. These synergistic effects position the developed CDs as promising candidates for anti-counterfeiting systems, multilevel data encryption, and high-security optical tagging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Afterglow materials absorb and store excitation energy, subsequently releasing photons through delayed emission after the excitation source is terminated. This phenomenon results in the material exhibiting self-luminescence for a certain period. Owing to this unique time-resolved optical property, afterglow materials exhibit functional versatility compared to conventional fluorescent systems, with applications spanning emergency signage, optical encryption, displays, and bioimaging [1]. Afterglow materials, distinguished by their unique spectral signatures resistant to replication, facile activation protocols, and low instrumental demands [2–4], serve as critical components in applications including latent fingerprint detection, covert authentication in secure documents, and brand protection systems [5,6]. Afterglow luminescent materials are primarily classified into room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) [7]. These materials achieve extended afterglow durations by utilizing triplet excitons in long-lived excited states and mitigating inherently constrained electron transitions (spin-forbidden processes) [8,9]. However, many afterglow materials rely on rare-earth elements, organometallics, or organic-based systems, presenting challenges including elevated production costs, toxicity risks, and intricate synthetic protocols [10,11]. The development of high-performance, eco-friendly afterglow materials remains pivotal, with carbon dots (CDs) emerging as tunable nanoluminescent systems due to their biocompatibility and programmable optical properties, positioning them as versatile candidates for advanced photonic applications [12,13]. However, current studies on CDs have primarily emphasized their fluorescent characteristics, whereas afterglow emission mechanisms and associated applications remain underexplored. The afterglow effect in CDs is typically achieved by embedding them within specific host matrices. Common matrices include hydrogels, polyvinyl alcohol, aluminum oxide, sodium hydroxide, melamine, boric acid, and silica [14–20]. The afterglow emission in CDs is generally induced through hydrogen bonding, covalent interactions, ionic bonding forces, or the combined effects of multiple stabilization mechanisms. For instance, Liu et al. demonstrated this phenomenon by incorporating PM-CDs into a boric acid matrix via covalent bonding interactions [21]. Furthermore, CDs can exhibit enhanced afterglow emission through strengthened spin–orbit coupling (SOC), a phenomenon amplified by the heavy-atom effect [22]. Fluorine, a traditional heavy atom, can reduce the energy gap between the singlet and triplet states, thereby enhancing the intersystem crossing (ISC) rate and strengthening SOC through mechanisms analogous to the internal heavy-atom effect [23]. Notably, fluorine reduces the energy gap between the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO, respectively), facilitating intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) and optimizing the photoluminescent properties of CDs [10]. Furthermore, the high electronegativity of fluorine facilitates the formation of robust inter- and intra-dot hydrogen-bonding networks. These hydrogen-bonding networks establish a rigid structural environment that suppresses non-radiative molecular vibrations, thereby extending the lifetimes of CDs [24]. Moreover, metal ions exhibit a higher electron count, unfilled orbitals, and larger atomic radii compared to nonmetallic elements. Doping CDs with metal ions enhances SOC and ISC efficiency, thereby optimizing afterglow-emission performance [25]. Notably, efficient blue-light emission has been achieved in CDs via Na doping; however, the influence of Na doping on their afterglow-emission properties remains underexplored [26].

In this study, we present a synthesis strategy for CDs with dual-mode afterglow characteristics via fluorine and sodium co-doping. Our results demonstrate that fluorine doping regulates emission wavelength and afterglow decay, while sodium doping critically enhances both afterglow duration and luminous intensity. Furthermore, the three-dimensional network architecture in the matrix functions as a rigid scaffold, suppressing non-radiative decay pathways by restricting vibrational and rotational freedom of CDs chromophores. These tailored optical properties enable sophisticated applications such as multilevel data encryption, interference-resistant fingerprint visualization, and dynamic information encryption. Notably, this study introduces a simple and effective strategy for fabricating CDs with prolonged afterglow durations, while also elucidating their underlying mechanisms and potential applications across various fields.

2,4 Difluorobenzoic acid, Benzoic acid, Diethanolamine, Sodium hydroxide, Boric acid and all other chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co. Ltd and all the experiments were carried out without further purification.

2,4-Difluorobenzoic acid (10 mmol) and diethanolamine (13 mmol) were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water in a 100 mL beaker and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon-lined) autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting solution was cooled to room temperature and filtered using a PTFE syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. For further purification, the filtered solution was dialyzed in deionized water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da for 24 h. The purified solution was then freeze-dried to obtain F,N-CDs. N-CDs were prepared using fluorine-free benzoic acid as the carbon source, following the same method.

Prepare a 100 ml beaker containing 3 g of boric acid (BA), 80 mg of sodium hydroxide, and 40 mL of F,N-CDs solution was prepared. The beaker was sealed with aluminum foil to minimize water evaporation, and the reaction was conducted hydrothermally at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was complete, the product was ground in a mortar to obtain CDs-1. The product without sodium hydroxide was labeled CDs-3, while the N-CDs were denoted as CDs-2.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using a Rigaku D/MAX 2500 V instrument, while Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured with a Shimadzu IRTracer-100. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI from Thermo Fisher Scientific. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-3600Plus spectrophotometer. Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra, including lifetimes, were measured using an FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer from Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK. Afterglow times were measured using a 3D-TSL system. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) were performed using a Shimadzu DTG-60 and a DSC-214, respectively. Variable-temperature fluorescence was measured with an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrophotometer, and digital images were captured using an iMX800 instrument.

This work investigates the effect of F and Na elemental doping on the properties of afterglow CDs. Blue-fluorescent F,N-doped seed CDs (F,N-CDs) were synthesized in a hydrothermal process, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Diethanolamine functioned as the nitrogen source, and 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid served as the carbon source, thereby facilitating simultaneous nitrogen and fluorine doping. Additionally, boric acid served as a precursor for the rigid matrix, while sodium hydroxide (NaOH) provided the sodium source. Carboxylic groups on the carbon dot surfaces undergo ionization upon exposure to sodium hydroxide, resulting in the formation of numerous ionic bonds. These ionic bonds, combined with hydrogen and covalent bonds, immobilize the carbon dots within a rigid network, thereby reducing non-radiative attenuation and facilitating long-lasting afterglow emission.

The synthesis of afterglow carbon dots (CDs) comprises two distinct stages: the synthesis of seed CDs and their subsequent immobilization onto the substrate, yielding fluorine-doped and sodium-doped CDs, respectively. Fluorine and nitrogen co-doped seed CDs (F,N-CDs) exhibit a light brown coloration under ambient conditions and display blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation (Fig. S1). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of F,N-CDs (Fig. 1 a) demonstrate uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical particles in aqueous solution, with sizes distributed within a 2–8 nm range and an average diameter of approximately 4.46 nm (Fig. 1c). High-resolution TEM images of F,N-CDs (Fig. 1b) show a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [27]. The F,N-CDs were incorporated into a rigid matrix to form a transparent glass-like composite. Upon grinding, this material produced a light-yellow powder exhibiting distinct afterglow properties, designated as CDs-1. To evaluate the crystallinity of CDs-1, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed. The XRD pattern (Fig. 1d) displays two broad diffraction peaks at 25.3° and 41.3°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [28,29]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern shows two sharp peaks at 14.5° and 28.3°, indicating the presence of B2O3 diffraction peaks, consistent with the standard diffraction card of PWO [30]. Additionally, a distinct peak at 19.8° is attributed to NaOH, confirmed by the XRD pattern of a carbon dot-free matrix prepared under identical conditions. These findings indicate that B2O3 formed via dehydration of boric acid during carbonization, resulting in the encapsulation of F,N-CDs within a composite matrix comprising B2O3 and NaOH [31]. The surface functional groups and chemical composition of CDs-1 were further analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1e) reveals the surface functional groups of CDs. A broad absorption peak in the 3200–3500 cm−1 range corresponds to OH/NH stretching vibrations on the CDs' surface [32]. Peaks at 1690 cm−1 and 1619 cm−1 are attributed to CN and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [33]. Additional peaks at 1436 cm−1, 1306 cm−1, and 925 cm−1 correspond to BN stretching, BOB asymmetric stretching, and BC stretching vibrations, respectively [34]. Furthermore, CDs-1 and CDs-3 exhibit peaks at 1137 cm−1 and 1094 cm−1, corresponding to semi-ionic CF and covalent CF bonds, respectively [35]. As CDs-2 lacks fluorine, these characteristic peaks are absent. These findings demonstrate the incorporation of fluorine, nitrogen, and boron into seed CDs, alongside their stable immobilization onto the substrate via hydrogen-bond networks, covalent bonding, and semi-ionic interactions. The XPS spectra of CDs (Fig. 1f) confirmed their chemical composition. Spectral analysis indicated that fluorine-free CDs-2 was successfully doped with sodium, while CDs-3 achieved fluorine doping. CDs-1 exhibited effective co-doping with fluorine and sodium, with atomic percentages of C (32.37 %), O (43.66 %), B (15.70 %), N (4.51 %), F (1.97 %), and Na (1.79 %). It can be observed that the mild fluorine doping via the hydrothermal method results in a relatively low degree of incubation. Additionally, co-doping with fluorine and nitrogen enhances the number of triplet excitons [15]. These findings establish the successful synthesis of precursor carbon dots (CDs) and their integration into the B2O3/NaOH composite matrix. In contrast to conventional methodologies, this strategy introduces multiple confinement effects on the immobilized CDs, thereby stabilizing the triplet excited state and achieving long-afterglow emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: F, Na doping enhances afterglow and fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: The photophysical properties of CDs were thoroughly characterized. Digital photographs of CDs-1 before and after 365 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 2 a. Upon excitation at 365 nm, CDs-1 emitted bright greenish-white luminescence. When the excitation light was turned off, CDs-1 displayed a brighter green afterglow lasting approximately 14 s, longer than the 8 s afterglow of CDs-2 and 12-second afterglow of CDs-3 (Fig. S2). The fluorescence quantum yields of CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3 were 34.27 %, 13.06 %, and 30.33 %, respectively Fig. S3). To more precisely evaluate the afterglow duration, the afterglow intensity versus time curves of CDs-1 through CDs-3 were measured using a multifunctional defect fluorescence spectrometer (Fig. 2b). The results indicate that the afterglow times of CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3 are 40 s, 38 s, and 26 s, respectively. Fluorine-doped CDs demonstrate substantially prolonged afterglow durations relative to their undoped counterparts, while sodium doping exerts only a limited impact on afterglow persistence. Notably, sodium-doped CDs-1 exhibit an 11.3 % enhancement in afterglow intensity compared to sodium-free CDs-3, albeit with an accelerated decay rate. To systematically investigate the effects of sodium doping on the photophysical properties of CDs, a series of sodium-doped CDs (CDs-xNa) were synthesized with controlled sodium hydroxide (NaOH) concentrations. The afterglow times, shown in Fig. 2c, reveal a trend of initially increasing followed by decreasing, with the longest afterglow observed at a NaOH doping level of 80 mg (CDs-1). The prolonged afterglow duration is primarily attributed to sodium doping-induced suppression of nonradiative decay pathways. Conversely, elevated NaOH doping concentrations increase the reaction system’s alkalinity, inducing structural degradation in the seed CDs and resulting in diminished afterglow persistence and emission intensity. The photophysical properties of CDs-1 were further characterized. As shown in the decay curve in d, the average lifetime of CDs-1 is 0.574 s (Fig. 2d), which is significantly longer than the 194.61 ms observed for CDs-2 (Fig. S4). This result is consistent with the measured afterglow duration. The variable excitation fluorescence spectra of the seed carbon dots (F,N-CDs) in CDs-1 (Fig. 2e) demonstrate fluorescence tunability within the excitation range of 280 nm to 440 nm. The emission intensity initially increases, then decreases, exhibiting an excitation-dependent emission behavior after 340 nm. This phenomenon may result from the presence of multiple emission centers, which shift as the excitation wavelength increases. The change in dominant emission centers beyond 340 nm is likely due to inhomogeneous emission from the surface states of oxygen-containing functional groups [36]. When the excitation wavelength is 320 nm, the fluorescence intensity is the strongest. The excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of CDs-1, shown in Fig. 2f, exhibit stable emission characteristics in the excitation range from 280 nm to 380 nm. With increasing excitation wavelength, two distinct emission peaks emerge at 406 nm and 523 nm. This behavior contrasts with F,N-CDs, which is ascribed to altered surface states arising from composite formation between F,N-CDs and the matrix, thereby generating additional emissive centers. These findings suggest that the observed spectral changes are attributable to fluorine-containing functional groups on the seed CD surfaces. Fig. 2g displays the excitation-dependent afterglow spectra of CDs-1. Analogous to the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of F,N-CDs, the emission intensity within the 300–380 nm range initially increases before diminishing, though no excitation-dependent spectral shifting is observed. The fluorescence intensity is strongest when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. At this point, the emission peaks are located at 423 nm and 531 nm, with CIE coordinates of (0.22, 0.23) (Fig. S5). These findings establish that fluorine- and sodium-doped CDs-1 possess superior afterglow properties, with dual-doped elements enhancing emission via distinct mechanistic pathways.

Further detailed characterization was conducted to better understand the impact of doping on the optical properties of the CDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs were measured (Fig. 3 a). The results show that the fluorine-free CDs-2 exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, corresponding to the π–π* transitions of the CC bonds [37]. In contrast, the UV absorption of fluorine-doped CDs-1 and CDs-3 is similar, with strong absorption from the ultraviolet to the green region, corresponding to the n–π* transitions of CO/CN bonds [38]. Additionally, fluorine doping causes a redshift in the excitation wavelength of the CDs, from 340 nm to 360 nm. To further analyze the bonding environment of the CDs, high-resolution C 1s spectra were characterized. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of CDs-1, shown in Fig. 3b, exhibits four peaks at 283.8 eV (CF), 284.5 eV (CO/CN), 286.2 eV (CC/CC), and 286.4 eV (CO). The high-resolution B 1s spectrum of CDs-1 (Fig. 3c) shows three peaks at 190.6 eV (BCO2), 192.2 eV (BO), and 193.4 eV (B2O3). The high-resolution F 1s spectrum of CDs-1 (Fig. 3d) reveals two peaks at 687.3 eV (Covalent CF) and 685.4 eV (Semi-ionic CF) [23,35,39]. By comparing the content of chemical bonds in all CDs, it was found that the amount of CO significantly changed (Fig. 3e). Studies indicate that the degree of oxidation has a notable impact on the optical properties of CDs, as the singlet and triplet states are energetically close, facilitating SOC and ISC [39]. This phenomenon critically governs afterglow generation. To elucidate the emission mechanism of CDs-1, variable-temperature fluorescence spectroscopy analysis was systematically conducted. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the green emission peak at 523 nm remained spectrally stable over the 80–300 K temperature range, whereas the blue emission peak at 424 nm demonstrated a minor blue shift. This phenomenon is likely attributable to thermally amplified molecular vibrational/rotational energy, thereby elevating photon energy during electronic transitions and inducing a blue shift in the emission peak. Fig. 3g presents the temperature-dependent emission intensity profiles of the green and blue emission bands. Both peaks exhibit an initial intensity increase followed by a decrease with elevated temperatures, displaying inflection points at 180 K and 260 K, respectively. However, in general, fluorescence intensity tends to decrease with increasing temperature due to the enhanced molecular vibrations. This diminution arises from internal conversion processes, wherein excitation energy is dissipated as vibrational energy via rapid non-radiative relaxation in the ground state. Consequently, accelerated non-radiative decay pathways dominate, thereby suppressing fluorescence emission efficiency. The observed emission enhancement in CDs-1 with increasing temperature confirms the presence of TADF in its afterglow mechanism. Furthermore, the delayed thermal inflection point of the “blue peak” compared to the “green peak” suggests a dominant TADF contribution in the blue spectral region. The emission mechanism of CDs-1 was further investigated through delayed-time afterglow spectra (Fig. 3h). The fluorescence emission peaks were observed at 406 nm and 523 nm, while the afterglow peaks appeared at 420 nm and 531 nm. The calculated ΔEST values were 0.130 eV and 0.036 eV, respectively. The very small ΔEST values for both the “blue peak” and “green peak” are favorable for RISC. Combined with the changes observed in the temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra, these results confirm the presence of TADF in CDs-1. Additionally, the afterglow spectra at longer delay times, as shown in Fig. S6, exhibit an emission peak at 532 nm, corresponding to the visually observed green afterglow. The results indicate that the emission process of CDs-1 follows the mechanism illustrated in Fig. 3i. Upon excitation, electrons transition from the ground state to the singlet excited state, followed by relaxation to S1 and subsequent return to the ground state, producing blue-green fluorescence. Moreover, the small energy gap between S1 and T1 (ΔEST = 0.036 eV) facilitates both ISC and RISC, ultimately resulting in afterglow emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The practicality of CDs composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application potential of afterglow CDs, the stability characteristics of the CDs powder were systematically assessed. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the impact of environmental conditions such as humidity and UV exposure on CDs-1 powder was examined under various experimental setups. Fig. 4b illustrates the mass variation trends of CDs-1 and CDs-2 under identical open conditions over time. It was observed that the mass of CDs-1 remained consistently lower than that of CDs-2 during the 0–10 day testing period. This enhanced stability is predominantly observed in CDs-1, which contain CF bonds. These covalent bonds exhibit high bond dissociation energy and reduced surface free energy, conferring exceptional hydrophobicity to the fluorine-doped carbon dot system. Following a 10-day period, both materials attained mass equilibrium, exhibiting minor fluctuations within a narrow range, which suggests the establishment of hygroscopic equilibrium under the specified experimental conditions. Notably, ambient humidity variations induced measurable mass fluctuations in the material. Concurrently, the afterglow duration of CDs-1 decreased from 14 s to 8 s under these hygroscopic conditions. To assess the light-bleaching resistance of the CDs, the samples were exposed to a stable UV light source, and their afterglow duration was recorded hourly. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the afterglow durations of the CDs decreased by 56.43 %, 71.43 %, and 56.67 %, respectively. Notably, CDs-2, which lacked fluorine doping, exhibited significantly greater attenuation than the fluorine-doped CDs-1 and CDs-3. To evaluate the thermal stability of the CDs, thermogravimetric (TG) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analyses were conducted. As shown in Fig. 4d, the TG curve indicates that CDs-1 lost approximately 5 % of its mass at 150 °C, demonstrating its relatively high stability. The DSC curve (Fig. 4e) presents the glass transition temperatures (Tg) of CDs-1, CDs-2, and CDs-3 at 70 °C, 65 °C, and 58 °C, respectively, suggesting a gradual reduction in the matrix's constraint on the CDs. This trend aligns with the observed variations in afterglow duration. These findings indicate that CDs-1 exhibits superior thermal stability compared to CDs-2 and CDs-3, as well as enhanced photothermal stability and resistance to water and oxygen quenching due to the matrix. Finally, Fig. 4f compares the afterglow duration and emission wavelength of the CDs in this study with those of similar systems, highlighting that CDs-1 achieves the longest afterglow time [40–46].

Finally, the distinct photophysical properties of CDs-1 to CDs-3, along with their visible differences, indicate their potential applications in information encryption. Utilizing the bright and prolonged afterglow characteristics of CDs-1, we further demonstrate its capability for fingerprint detection and identification. The finely ground CDs-1 powder was evenly applied to plastic and glass surfaces containing potential fingerprints and left undisturbed for a designated period. Subsequently, excess powder was carefully removed. Digital images captured using an iMX800 sensor (Fig. 5 a and Fig. S7) under different lighting conditions reveal that fingerprint features are obscured under both natural light and 365 nm UV light due to interference. However, when UV excitation is turned off, the fingerprint image becomes significantly clearer. Furthermore, experimental results indicate that CDs-1 powder exhibits superior performance in fingerprint recognition on smooth surfaces, such as plastic and glass, compared to rough surfaces like paper and wood. Finally, to fully utilize the fluorescence and afterglow properties of this series of CDs, they were applied in Morse code-based information encryption. A full circle represented the “-” signal, while a half-circle denoted the “·” signal, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. All circles and half-circles were fabricated by mixing corn starch with the corresponding CDs in a specific ratio, followed by compression of the mixture. Fig. 5c depicts the information encryption response process. Initially, under natural light, all markers are visible, displaying the information “carbondot.” When exposed to 365 nm UV light, pure corn starch reflects minimal light, whereas markers containing CDs exhibit noticeable luminescence, altering the displayed information to “cardot.” Once the UV light is turned off, markers with seed CDs cease to emit light, and the displayed information further reduces to “cart.” Finally, as the afterglow of CDs-3 gradually fades, markers containing the mixed CDs-1 continue to emit light, revealing the true information “cat.” Fig. 5d shows a digital photograph of the information encryption using Morse code. In continuous shooting mode, markers were successfully applied to the information encryption response procedure outlined in Fig. 5c, using different excitation light sources and exposure times. The only limitation is the variation in the emission wavelengths of the CDs, which causes differences in marker colors. However, the current results demonstrate the potential application of CDs in information encryption.

In summary, we synthesized CDs with multiple heteroatom doping, achieving a lifetime and quantum yield of 0.574 s and 34.27 %, respectively, for CDs-1. By embedding seed CDs into a rigid crystal network through the combined constraints of hydrogen, covalent, and ionic bonds, efficient dual-mode afterglow emission was realized. Fluorine doping effectively reduced the ΔEST to 0.036 eV, significantly promoting ISC/RISC processes and markedly increasing the afterglow duration. Additionally, sodium doping enhanced the initial afterglow intensity by 11.3 % and slightly extended the emission duration. The CDs demonstrated excellent practicality, showcasing their potential applications in fingerprint recognition and information encryption based on their distinct afterglow properties. This study provides valuable insights into the development of high-performance afterglow CDs.

Wei Gao: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hao Hu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Jinkun Xue: Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation. Yongrun Dong: Investigation, Methodology. Wen Li: Formal analysis. Xia Zhao: Methodology, Software. Xiang Mao: Investigation, Methodology. Zequan Li: Methodology, Project administration, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AD23026201), and by the Industry-university Research Cooperation Fund of Guangxi Hongkai Furniture Co., Ltd & Guangxi University (20200782).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126259.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
